source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There was a certain man in Cæsarea called Cornelius, a centurion of the band called the Italian band, a devout man, and one that feared God with all his house, which gave much alms to the people, and prayed to God alway.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saw in a vision evidently about the ninth hour of the day an angel of God coming in to him, and saying unto him, Cornelius.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he looked on him, he was afraid, and said, What is it, Lord?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he said unto him, Thy prayers and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This man is not a Jew, nor of those under the Law, but he had already forestalled our manner of life.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, thus far, two persons, both of high rank, receiving the faith, the eunuch at Gaza and this man; and the pains taken on behalf of these men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But do not imagine that this was because of their high rank: God forbid! it was because of their piety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that the Scripture mentions their dignified stations, is to show the greatness of their piety; since it is more wonderful when a person being in a position of wealth and power is such as these were.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What makes the praise of the former is, his undertaking so long a journey, and this when there was no (festival) season to require it, and his reading on his road, and while riding in his chariot, and his beseeching Philip, and numberless other points: and the great praise of the latter is, that he makes alms and prayers, and is a just man, holding such a command.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The reason why the writer describes the man so fully, is, that none may say that the Scripture history relates falsehoods: “Cornelius,” he says, “a centurion of the band called the Italian band.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A “band,” σπεἵρα, is what we now call a “numerous.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A devout man,” he says, “and one that feared God with all his house” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2): that you may not imagine that it is because of his high station that these things are done.—When Paul was to be brought over, there is no angel, but the Lord Himself: and He does not send him to some great one, but to a very ordinary person: but here, on the contrary, He brings the chief Apostle (to these Gentiles), not sends them to him: herein condescending to their weakness, and knowing how such persons need to be treated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As indeed on many occasions we find Christ Himself hasting (to such), as being more infirm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or (it may be) because (Cornelius) was not able himself to leave his home.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here again is a high commendation of alms, just as was there given by means of Tabitha.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A devout man,” it says, “and one that feared God with all his house.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us hear this, whoever of us neglect them of our own house, whereas this man was careful of his soldiers also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And that gave alms,” it says, “to all the people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both his doctrines and his life were right.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He saw in a vision evidently, about the ninth hour of the day, an angel of God coming in to him, and saying unto him, Cornelius.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why does he see the angel?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This also was in order to the full assurance of Peter, or rather, not of him, but of the others, the weaker ones.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At the ninth hour,” when he was released from his cares and was at quiet, when he was engaged in prayers and compunction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he looked on him, he was afraid.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how what the angel speaks he does not speak immediately, but first rouses and elevates his mind.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: At the sight, there was fear, but a fear in moderation, just so far as served to fix his attention.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also the words relieved him of his fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The fear roused him: the praise mitigated what was unpleasant in the fear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thy prayers,” saith he, “and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now send men to Joppa, and call for one Simon, whose surname is Peter.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lest they should come to a different person, he designates the man not only by his surname, but by the place.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the same,” saith he, “is lodging with one Simon a tanner, who hath his house by the seaside.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how the Apostles, for love of solitude and quiet, affected the retired quarters of the cities?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With one Simon a tanner:” how then if it chanced that there was another?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold, there is another token, his dwelling by the seaside.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All three tokens could not possibly coincide (elsewhere).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He does not tell him for what purpose, that he may not take off the intense desire, but he leaves him to an eager and longing expectation of what he shall hear.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when the Angel which spake unto Cornelius was departed, he called two of his household servants, and a devout soldier of them that waited on him continually; and when he had declared all these things unto them, he sent them to Joppa.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see, that it is not without purpose that the writer says this? (it shows) that those also “who waited on him continually” were such as he.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when he had declared the whole matter unto them:” observe the unassuming character of the man: for he does not say, Call Peter to me: but, in order also to induce him to come, he declared the whole matter:—this was so ordered by Providence;—for he did not choose to use the authority of his rank to fetch Peter to him; therefore “he declared the matter;” such was the moderation of the man: and yet no great notion was to be formed of one lodging with a tanner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And on the morrow, as they journeyed, and drew nigh to the city” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9.)—observe how the Spirit connects the times: no sooner than this, and no later, He Causes this to take place—“Peter about the sixth hour went up upon the housetop to pray:” that is, privately and quietly, as in an upper chamber.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he became very hungry, and would have eaten; but while they made ready, there fell upon him a trance.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What means this expression, ἔκστασις, “trance?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Rather, there was presented to him a kind of spiritual view (θεωρία): the soul, so to say, was caused to be out of the body (ἐξέστη).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And saw heaven opened, and, knit at the four corners, a certain vessel descending unto him, as it had been a great sheet, and let down to the earth: wherein were all manner of fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And there came a voice to him, Rise, Peter; kill, and eat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Peter said, Not so, Lord; for I have never eaten anything that is common or unclean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the voice spake unto him again the second time, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was done thrice: and the vessel was received up again into heaven.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a symbol of the whole world.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was uncircumcised: and —for he had nothing in common with the Jews—they would all accuse him as a transgressor: “thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them: (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3)” this was a thing altogether offensive to them: observe then what is providentially managed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He himself also says, “I have never eaten:” not being himself afraid—far be the thought from us—but it is so contrived by the Spirit, in order that he may have it to say in answer to those accusing him, that he did object: for it was altogether necessary for them to observe the Law.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He was in the act of being sent to the Gentiles: therefore that these also may not accuse him, see how many things are contrived (by the Providence of God).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, that it may not seem to be a mere fancy, “this was done thrice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I said,” saith he, “Not so, Lord, for I have never eaten aught common or unclean.—And the voice came unto him, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common.” (ch. xi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It seems indeed to be spoken to him, but the whole is meant for the Jews.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the teacher is rebuked, much more these.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The earth then, this is what the linen sheet denotes, and the wild beasts in it, are they of the Gentiles, and the command, “Kill and eat,” denotes that he must go to them also; and that this thing is thrice done, denotes baptism.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What God hath cleansed,” saith it, “call not thou common.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great daring!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore did he object?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That none may say that God was proving him, as in the case of Abraham, this is why he says, “Not so, Lord,” etc. not gainsaying—just as to Philip also He said, “How many loaves have ye?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not to learn, but tempting, or “proving him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it was the same (Lord) that had discoursed above (in the Law) concerning things clean and unclean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But in that sheet were also “all the four-footed beasts of the earth:” the clean with the unclean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And for all this, he knew not what it meant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now while Peter doubted in himself what this vision which he had seen should mean, behold, the men which were sent from Cornelius had made enquiry for Simon’s house, and stood before the gate, and called, and asked whether Simon, which was surnamed Peter, were lodged there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But while Peter,” it says, “doubted in himself” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17, 18), the men come at the right moment to solve his doubt: just as (the Lord) suffered Joseph first to be perturbed in mind, and then sends the Angel: for the soul with ease accepts the solution, when it has first been in perplexity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His perplexity neither lasts long (when it did occur), nor (did it occur) before this, but just at the moment when they “asked whether he were lodging there.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: While Peter thought on the vision, the Spirit said unto him, Behold, three men seek thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Arise therefore, and get thee down, and go with them, doubting nothing: for I have sent them.” (supra, p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 142, and 145, note 7; v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this again is a plea for Peter in answer to the disciples, that he did doubt, and was instructed to doubt nothing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I,” saith He, “have sent them.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great is the authority of the Spirit!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What God doth, this the Spirit is said to do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so the Angel, but having first said, “Thy prayers and thine alms have ascended, for a memorial before God,” to show that he is sent from thence, then he adds, “And now send men,” etc.: the Spirit not so, but, “For I have sent them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Peter went down to the men which were sent unto him from Cornelius; and said, Behold, I am he whom ye seek: what is the cause wherefore ye are come?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they said, Cornelius the centurion, a just man, and one that feareth God and of good report among all the nation of the Jews, was warned from God by an holy angel to send for thee into his house, and to hear words of thee.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They speak his praises, so as to persuade him that an Angel has in fact appeared unto him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then called he them in,” (b) that they may suffer no harm, “and lodged them:” thenceforth he without scruple takes his meals with them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And on the morrow Peter went away with them, and certain brethren from Cæsarea accompanied him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the morrow after, they entered into Cæsarea.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The man was a person of note, and it was in a city of note that he then was.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (a) But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “There was a certain man in Cæsarea,” etc. (Recapitulation, v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe with whom the beginning of the Gentiles is made—with “a devout man,” and one proved to be worthy by his works.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if, though the case be so, they are still offended, if this had not been the case, what would not have been the consequence!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But mark the greatness of the assurance. (c) To this end all is done (in the way it is done), and the affair takes its beginning from Judea. (d) “He saw in a vision, evidently,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not in his sleep that the Angel appeared to him, but while he was awake, in the daytime, “about the ninth hour.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saw an Angel of God coming in unto him, and saying unto him, Cornelius.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when he looked on him, he was afraid.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So occupied was he with himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Implying, that it was in consequence of the Angel’s calling him by a voice that he saw him; as, had he not called him, he would not have seen him: so taken up was he with the act in which he was engaged.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the Angel says to him, “Thy prayers and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God, and now send men to Joppa, and call for one Simon, who is called Peter.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So far, he signified that the sending for him would be for good consequences, but in what way good, he did not intimate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So, neither does Peter relate the whole matter, but everywhere, the narratives are in part only, for the purpose of making the hearers apply their minds to what is said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Send and call for Simon:” in like manner the Angel only calls Philip.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as they went on their journey, and drew nigh to the city” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9): in order that Peter should not be in perplexity too long.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Peter went up upon the housetop,” etc. Observe, that not even his hunger forced him to have recourse to the sheet.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Rise, Peter,” saith the Voice, “kill and eat.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably he was on his knees when he saw the vision.—To me it seems that this also denotes the Gospel (or, “the Preaching”).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That the thing taking place was of God (the circumstances made evident, namely), both that he sees it (descending) from above, and that he is in a trance; and, that the voice comes from thence, and the thrice confessing that the creatures there were unclean, and its coming from thence, and being drawn back thither (all this), is a mighty token of the cleanness (imparted to them).—But why is this done?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the sake of those thereafter, to whom he is about to relate it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For to himself it had been said, “Go not into the way of the Gentiles.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 5.) * * For if Paul needed both (to give) circumcision, and (to offer) sacrifice, much more (was some assurance needed) then, in the beginning of the Preaching, while they were as yet weaker.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 16.)—Observe too how he did not at once receive them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, it says, they “called, and asked, whether Simon, which was surnamed Peter, were lodging there.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As it was a mean looking house, they asked below, they inquired of the neighbors.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And while Peter thought, the Spirit said unto him, Arise, get thee down, and go, nothing doubting, for I have sent them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he does not say, For to this end did the vision appear unto thee; but, “I have sent them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Peter went down” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21)—this is the way the Spirit must be obeyed, without demanding reasons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is sufficient for all assurance to be told by Him, This do, this believe: nothing more (is needed)—“Then Peter went down, and said, Behold, I am he whom ye seek: what is the cause wherefore ye are come?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He saw a soldier, saw a man: it was not that he was afraid, on the contrary, having first confessed that he was the person whom they sought, then he asks for the cause (of their coming); that it may not be supposed that the reason of his asking the cause, was, that he wished to hide himself: (he asks it) in order, that if it be immediately urgent, he may also go forth with them, but if not, may receive them as guests.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And they said, etc. into his house.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This he had ordered them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not think he has done this out of contempt: not as of contempt has he sent, but so he was ordered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Cornelius was waiting for them, and had called together his kinsmen and near friends.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was right that his kinsmen and friends should be gathered to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But being there present, they would have heard from him (what had happened).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how great the virtue of alms, both in the former discourse, and here!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There, it delivered from death temporal; here, from death eternal; and opened the gates of heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such are the pains taken for the bringing of Cornelius to the faith, that both an angel is sent, and the Spirit works, and the chief of the Apostles is fetched to him, and such a vision is shown, and, in short, nothing is left undone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many centurions were there not besides, and tribunes, and kings, and none of them obtained what this man did!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hear, all ye that are in military commands, all ye that stand beside kings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “A just man,” it says, “fearing God; devout” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2, and 22); and what is more than all, with all his house.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not as we (who): that our servants may be afraid of us, do everything. but not that they may be devout.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And over the domestics too, so * *.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not so this man; but he was “one that feared God with all his house” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 2), for he was as the common father of those with him, and of all the others (under his command.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe what (the soldier) says himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, fearing * *, he adds this also: “well reported of by all the nation.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what if he was uncircumcised?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, but those give him a good report.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing like alms: great is the virtue of this practice, when the alms is poured forth from pure stores; for it is like a fountain discharging mud, when it issues from unjust stores, but when from just gains, it is as a limpid and pure stream in a paradise, sweet to the sight, sweet to the touch, both light and cool, when given in the noon-day heat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such is alms.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Beside this fountain, not poplars and pines, nor cypresses, but other plants than these, and far better, of goodly stature: friendship with God, praise with men, glory to Godward, good-will from all; blotting out of sins, great boldness, contempt of wealth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the fountain by which the plant of love is nourished: for nothing is so wont to nourish love, as the being merciful: it makes its branches to lift themselves on high.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This fountain is better than that in Paradise (Gen. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10); a fountain, not dividing into four heads, but reaching unto Heaven itself: this gives birth to that river “which springeth up into eternal life” (John iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14): on this let Death light, and like a spark it is extinguished by the fountain: such, wherever it drops, are the mighty blessings it causes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This quenches, even as a spark, the river of fire: this so strangles that worm, as naught else can do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that has this, shall not gnash his teeth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of the water of this, let there be dropped upon the chains, and it dissolves them: let it but touch the firebrands, it quenches all.—A fountain does not give out streams for a while and anon run dry,—else must it be no more a fountain,—but ever gushes: so let our fountain give out more copiously of the streams of mercy (in alms).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This cheers him that receives: this is alms, to give out not only a copious, but a perennial, stream.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou wouldest that God rain down His mercy upon thee as from fountains, have thou also a fountain.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet there is no comparison (between God’s fountain and thine): for if thou open the mouths of this fountain, such are the mouths of God’s Fountain as to surpass every abyss.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: God does but seek to get an opportunity on our part, and pours forth from His storehouses His blessings.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When He expends, when He lavishes, then is He rich, then is He affluent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Large is the mouth of that fountain: pure and limpid its water.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou stop not up the fountain here, neither wilt thou stop up that fountain.—Let no unfruitful tree stand beside it, that it may not waste its spray.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Hast thou wealth?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Plant not poplars there: for such is luxury: it consumes much, and shows nothing for it in itself, but spoils the fruit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Plant not a pine-tree—such is wantonness in apparel, beautiful only to the sight, and useful for nothing—nor yet a fir-tree, nor any other of such trees as consume indeed, but are in no sort useful.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Set it thick with young shoots: plant all that is fruitful, in the hands of the poor, all that thou wilt.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing richer than this ground.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though small the reach of the hand, yet the tree it plants starts up to heaven and stands firm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This it is to plant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that which is planted on the earth will perish, though not now, at any rate a hundred years hence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou plantest many trees, of which thou shalt not enjoy the fruit, but ere thou canst enjoy it, death comes upon thee.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This tree will give thee its fruit then, when thou art dead.—If thou plant, plant not in the maw of gluttony, that the fruit end not in the draught-house: but plant thou in the pinched belly, that the fruit may start up to heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Refresh the straightened soul of the poor, lest thou pinch thine own roomy soul.—See you not, that the plants which are over-much watered at the root decay, but grow when watered in moderation?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also drench not thou thine own belly, that the root of the tree decay not: water that which is thirsty, that it may bear fruit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If thou water in moderation, the sun will not wither them, but if in excess, then it withers them: such is the nature of the sun.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In all things, excess is bad; wherefore let us cut it off, that we also may obtain the things we ask for.—Fountains, it is said, rise on the most elevated spots.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us be elevated in soul, and our alms will flow with a rapid stream: the elevated soul cannot but be merciful, and the merciful cannot but be elevated.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he that despises wealth, is higher than the root of evils.—Fountains are oftenest found in solitary places: let us withdraw our soul from the crowd, and alms will gush out with us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fountains, the more they are cleaned, the more copiously they flow: so with us, the more we spend, the more all good grows.—He that has a fountain, has nothing to fear: then neither let us be afraid.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed this fountain is serviceable to us for drink, for irrigation, for building, for everything.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing better than this draught: it is not possible for this to inebriate.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better to possess such a fountain, than to have fountains running with gold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Better than all gold-bearing soil is the soul which bears this gold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it advances us, not into these earthly palaces, but into those above.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The gold becomes an ornament to the Church of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of this gold is wrought “the sword of the Spirit” (Eph. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), the sword by which the dragon is beheaded.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From this fountain come the precious stones which are on the King’s head.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then let us not neglect so great wealth, but contribute our alms with largeness, that we may be found worthy of the mercy of God, by the grace and tender compassion of His only begotten Son, with Whom to the Father and Holy Ghost together be glory, dominion, honor, now and ever, world without end.
